ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mango osmanthus tea. Mapped to the broader 'tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301099) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for mango osmanthus flavoured tea. F04.A07 (Tea and tea-based beverages) is used as the closest defensible parent category. No more specific sub-code for flavoured tea drinks has been confirmed as valid in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy; a more specific code is not assigned to avoid inventing an unverified code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific flavoured tea beverage variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 ml (350 g), equivalent to a regular-sized cup of mango osmanthus tea as typically served at Singaporean bubble tea and speciality tea stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Mango Osmanthus Tea (Less Sugar, No Topping) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mango Osmanthus Tea (Less Sugar, No Topping), CR ID D08010072. All per-100g values are taken as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — sugar-sweetened fruit-flavoured tea beverage analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mango osmanthus tea. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the analogous category of lightly sweetened fruit-flavoured tea beverages with approximately 6g sugar/100g, referencing the University of Sydney GI Database category for dilute sugar-sweetened beverages. GL per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (22.2g for 350g serving) / 100 = 11.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.37g sugar/100g exceeding the 5g/100g SSB threshold; added sugar independently raises blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Despite low sodium (6 mg/100g), the added sugar content (6.37g/100g) in this 'less sugar' beverage exceeds the 5g/100g SSB threshold and independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance; individuals with hypertension should choose completely unsweetened osmanthus tea instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.37g sugar/100g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar absorbed rapidly without fibre modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage contains 6.37g sugar/100g with zero dietary fibre, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage that causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this drink and opt for plain unsweetened osmanthus tea.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk via dyslipidaemia and inflammatory pathways
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The added sugar load (6.37g/100g, approximately 22g per 350ml serving) in this beverage contributes to excess free sugar intake, which is associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit sugar-sweetened beverages and prefer unsweetened tea alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6.37g/100g) with zero fibre promotes systemic inflammation; no meaningful anti-inflammatory components present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Added sugar in this beverage promotes systemic inflammation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; plain unsweetened osmanthus tea would provide modest polyphenol benefit without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango Osmanthus Tea (Less Sugar, No Topping) is a Singaporean speciality tea beverage made by brewing osmanthus flowers with tea and combining the infusion with mango-flavoured syrup at a reduced sugar level, served without any customisable toppings such as pearls or jelly. The drink has a light golden-amber colour with a delicate floral fragrance from the osmanthus blossoms and a mild tropical sweetness from the mango component. The texture is thin and watery, similar to a lightly sweetened iced tea. Per 100g, it provides 25 kcal, 6.35g carbohydrate, 6.37g sugar, 0g protein, 0g fat, 0g dietary fibre, and 6mg sodium. Despite the 'less sugar' designation, the beverage still contains 6.37g sugar per 100g (approximately 22g per standard 350ml serving), which classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB and WHO definitions. The absence of dietary fibre means that the sugar is absorbed rapidly, making this drink unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension. The low sodium content (6mg/100g) is a positive attribute, but it does not offset the sugar-related cardiovascular and metabolic concerns. This beverage is commonly consumed as a refreshing drink at Singaporean bubble tea shops and speciality tea stalls, and is popular as a breakfast or afternoon beverage. It is vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink mango osmanthus tea?
How many calories in mango osmanthus tea less sugar?
Is less sugar mango osmanthus tea suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of mango osmanthus tea?
Mango osmanthus tea nutrition facts Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D08010072) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. All per-100g values are taken as reported without adaptation.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; this is a sugar-sweetened tea beverage containing no protein-bearing ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; this beverage contains no fat-bearing ingredients by formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; the beverage is a liquid with no fibre-contributing solid components. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific mango osmanthus tea variant are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D08010072. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate based on lightly sweetened fruit-flavoured tea beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mango osmanthus tea was found in approved sources. The 'less sugar' designation does not bring this beverage below the SSB threshold of 5g sugar/100g; it remains classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage at 6.37g sugar/100g. FoodEx2 code F04.A07 (Tea and tea-based beverages) is used as the closest defensible parent; no more specific sub-code for flavoured tea drinks has been confirmed as valid in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy and a more granular code is not assigned to avoid inventing an unverified code.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (6.0), saturated_fat_g (0.0), and sugar_g (6.37) all present as numeric values consistent with HPB source data.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (52), gi_category (low), glycemic_load_per_serving (11.6), and gi_source all present. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database category for lightly sweetened fruit-flavoured tea beverages; estimation basis documented in gi_source and notes.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn mapped to FOODON_03301099 (tea beverage) with closest_parent match type and gap note. FoodEx2 mapped to F04.A07 (Tea and tea-based beverages) with closest_parent match type and gap note explaining why no more specific sub-code is assigned — avoids inventing an unverified code. SNOMED CT null with gap note. All mappings are defensible and documented; no codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and ingredient_synonyms populated.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes'.

Coverage includes English variants, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. Aliases are comprehensive and support multilingual retrieval.

Nutrient data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (approved primary source, exact match, CR ID D08010072). Zero macros justified as confirmed zero per HPB. All -1 sentinel fields documented. Singaporean multilingual naming requirements satisfied (English, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). All three required health conditions present with clinical guidance.
